critic.verdict: A well-structured chart with great rhythmic representation, but some hold-tap combinations and 8th-note syncopations push the physical demands slightly past a standard Easy difficulty.

issues.problem: Tapping an 8th-note L at 6.625 while holding U is highly complex for an Easy chart, requiring advanced independent foot coordination at 173 BPM.
Simultaneous HOLD L and tap U acts as a hold-jump, which is typically introduced in Medium charts rather than Easy.
An isolated 8th-note step (D to R in 0.173s) followed by a 4th-note (U) creates an off-beat syncopation that can easily trip up beginners.
Rapidly alternating holds with overlapping 8th-note taps (HOLD R with tap D, followed immediately by HOLD U with tap L) is far too physically demanding for this difficulty level.

critic.verdict: The chart features a fantastic representation of the song's syncopated Tresillo rhythm, but is held back by technical mapping errors and frustratingly tight jump-to-tap transitions.

issues.problem: A HOLD D is active during a JUMP LR at 13.203. This requires three panels to be pressed simultaneously on a single-dance chart, creating an impossible or highly uncomfortable physical conflict.
A 16th-note transition directly out of a JUMP LR into a tap D (86ms gap) at 173 BPM is extremely jarring to execute and highly prone to pad-misses or double-stepping.
Another extremely tight 16th-note transition immediately following a JUMP LR to a tap D, disrupting the flow of the song's drop.
A JUMP LR is placed exactly a 16th-note after tap U at 38.045, making the entry into the jump feel rushed and physically awkward at this tempo.

critic.verdict: Rhythmically accurate to the vocals but severely held back by extremely awkward physical flow, including numerous 16th-note jumps and spin-inducing runs.

issues.problem: Frequent 16th-note jumps (a jump occurring just ~86ms after a tap). This forces players to jump with one foot already in motion, causing severe balance issues and forcing bracket-steps/slides at 173 BPM.
Awkward 16th-note patterns (L-R-U-D and R-D-U-L) that force rapid spins or highly uncomfortable double-stepping at high speeds.
Over-prioritizing literal vocal syncopation at the cost of playability, leading to a stuttery chart that feels very unintuitive on pad.
